assertions.text: Discount ladder, and traveller eligibility are in separate tables. Rolling window counting as a third table is desirable.
assertions.evidence: The response has three tables: `reisDiscountLadderForAdultsAndSeniors` (discount ladder), `adultTicketPriceReflectsReisDiscount` (price calculation), and `childrenAlwaysReceiveFlatTwentyPercentDiscount` (children's flat discount). There is no dedicated traveller eligibility table comparing which passenger types get which discount type. There is also no rolling window counting table with boundary testing — the 30-day window appears only as a column header in the ladder tables.

assertions.text: Children's flat 20% discount is represented — either as a row in the eligibility table or a separate note. It does not follow the ladder.
assertions.evidence: The method `childrenAlwaysReceiveFlatTwentyPercentDiscount` with @Description: "Children always receive a flat 20% discount on single tickets, regardless of how many tickets have been purchased in the last 30 days." The value set `{0, 4, 10, 40}` on the tickets column explicitly demonstrates it does not follow the ladder.

assertions.text: The discount ladder has multiple tiers derived from the 'every fifth trip' rule, with boundary values or value sets showing which ticket counts produce which discount. The maximum 40% discount is represented.
assertions.evidence: The table `reisDiscountLadderForAdultsAndSeniors` shows rows at ticket counts 0, 4, 5, 9, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 55 with corresponding discounts 0,0,5,5,10,15,20,25,30,35,40,40 — multiple tiers with boundary values. The row "Maximum discount reached | {ADULT, SENIOR} | 40 | 40" and "Above maximum, still capped | {ADULT, SENIOR} | 55 | 40" represent the 40% cap.

assertions.text: The 30-day rolling window boundary is tested: a ticket at exactly 30 days is included, at 31 days is excluded.
assertions.evidence: No table tests the rolling window boundary. The phrase "Tickets in last 30 days" appears as a column header, but there is no test case that distinguishes a ticket at exactly 30 days (included) vs. 31 days (excluded). The rolling window semantics are not tested anywhere in the response.

assertions.text: Eligibility uses human-readable values (yes/no or equivalent) rather than raw true/false. TypeConverter or similar mechanism mentioned.
assertions.evidence: There is no eligibility table using yes/no values. Passenger types use enum values (ADULT, SENIOR, CHILD). No TypeConverter or TypeConverterSources is mentioned anywhere in the response.

assertions.text: Value sets in the tier table contain exactly the values that produce the same discount. No cross-tier contamination.
assertions.evidence: In `childrenAlwaysReceiveFlatTwentyPercentDiscount`, the value set `{0, 4, 10, 40}` all produce the same 20% flat discount for children. In `reisDiscountLadderForAdultsAndSeniors`, the value set `{ADULT, SENIOR}` on the passenger type column groups the two types that share the same ladder — both produce identical discounts for each ticket count. No values from different discount outcomes are mixed in the same value set.

assertions.text: @DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.
assertions.evidence: No @DisplayName annotations are present, but method names read as clear descriptive titles: `reisDiscountLadderForAdultsAndSeniors`, `adultTicketPriceReflectsReisDiscount`, `childrenAlwaysReceiveFlatTwentyPercentDiscount`.

assertions.text: @Description is present on at least the class or the tier method and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the discount ladder structure, eligibility rules, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: @Description on the tier method: "Adults and seniors accumulate a personal Reis discount based on how many single tickets they have purchased in the last 30 days, measured at the time of purchase. The discount starts at 5% from the fifth ticket onwards, increases by 5% per additional five tickets, and is capped at a maximum of 40%." This explains the business rule structure, not just the column names.

assertions.text: TypeConverterSources is used for at least one human-readable conversion (yes/no, or rolling window notation).
assertions.evidence: No @TypeConverterSources annotation appears anywhere in the response. No TypeConverter class is referenced or imported in the code.

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence:
All @Description annotations use triple-quoted text blocks, e.g., `@Description("""
            Adults and seniors accumulate a personal Reis discount based on how many single \
            tickets they have purchased in the last 30 days...\
            """)`. No string concatenation with + is used.
